coredata.dc:title: Developing innovative point-of-care electrochemical sensors empowered by cardiac troponin I-responsive nanocomposite materials

coredata.dc:description: This study integrates dopamine (DA) electropolymerization, epitope imprinting, and nanomaterials (i.e., gold nanoparticles (AuNP) and graphene quantum dots (GQD)) to develop three distinct electrochemical sensors (DA-AuNP-MIP, DA-GQD-MIP, and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP) for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI) with assessing their sensitivity, selectivity, and specificity profiles. The sensors exhibited high affinity for the target biomarker, enabling detection within a concentration range of 0.01–20 ng mL−1, with dissociation constants ranging from 2.7 to 69 pM. The successful fabrication of sensors was also confirmed through atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), fluorescence microscopy, contact angle measurements, cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The cTnI selectivity assessment of imprinted nanosensors compared to their non-imprinted counterparts at 0.01–2.5 ng mL−1 showed that the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor yielded a maximum imprinting factor of 5.9. A negligible cross-reactivity for DA-AuNP-MIP and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensors was found against glucose, adenovirus epitope, another cTnI epitope, bovine serum albumin (BSA), transferrin, and neuron-specific enolase (NSE). Epitope specificity at various concentrations (10–60 nM), concentration-dependent cTnI detection in human serum (0.01–10 ng mL−1) and sensor recovery rates (0.1–10 ng mL−1) in human serum were determined with the best-performing sensor (i.e., DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP). The sensor demonstrated very high epitope specificity in comparison to another cTnI epitope as well as excellent detection performances in human serum studies. The newly engineered epitope-mediated electrochemical sensing platform showcases considerable potential for precise and straightforward diagnosis of coronary artery diseases, aiming to materialize a cost-effective Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of electrochemical MIP sensors using DA as functional monomer, each decorated with different nanomaterials. The step-wise protocol: A) Bare surface, B) Template adsorbed (SAM) surface, C) DA electropolymerization (with GQDs or AuNPs or combination of both), D) Template removal process, E) Rebinding with cTnI (peptide and protein).

SWV (upper row) and CV (middle row) characterization of sensor fabrication from bare surface to template removal. Polymerization voltammograms (lower row) for the corresponding DA-nanocomposite sensors.
AFM phase images of the molecular imprinting process. Bare, SAM, templated MIP (T-MIP), template-free MIP (TF-MIP), and control polymer (NIP) of DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD sensors surfaces.

AFM height and phase images, with corresponding cross-sectional profile and RMS roughness of molecular imprinting process in air for SAM, templated DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP (T-MIP), template-free MIP (TF-MIP) and control polymer (NIP) surfaces. The RMS results are average of 10 cross-sectional data.
SEM micrographs of A) DA-GQD-MIP, B) DA-AuNP-MIP and C) DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP, D) DA-GQD-NIP, E) DA-AuNP-NIP and F) DA-AuNP-GQD-NIP and their magnified images.

Goniometer pictures of the A) bare surface, B) SAM, C) DA-GQD-MIP, D) DA-GQD-NIP, E) DA-AuNP-MIP, F) DA-AuNP-NIP, G) DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP, and H) DA-AuNP-GQD-NIP. I) Swelling degrees of templated MIPs and template-free NIPs for DA-AuNP-GQD, DA-GQD, DA-AuNP, SAM, and bare surfaces.

Epitope detection studies using three differently prepared nanocomposite MIP sensors. A) The studied concentration range with corresponding LOD for DA-nanocomposite electrochemical sensors. B) Binding isotherms of cTnI-Ep4 on DA-AuNP-MIP, DA-GQD-MIP, and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP fitted to Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir-Freundlich adsorption models. The relative signal percentages were calculated with the corresponding template-free MIP signal as a reference point.
Optimal time of protein binding to each individual DA-nanocomposite based sensor. Three different concentrations of cTnI were tested with three distinct incubations times.

Overall protein detection assays. A) The concentration-dependent protein detection with corresponding LODs for DA-nanocomposite electrochemical sensors. B) Binding isotherms of cTnI on DA-AuNP-MIP, DA-GQD-MIP, and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP fitted to Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir-Freundlich adsorption models. C) Overall efficacy synopsis where the investigation range, the LOD, determination coefficient (R2), dissociation constant (Kd), and model fitting for cTnI detection of each DA-nanomaterial-based sensor is depicted. The relative signal percentages were calculated with the corresponding template-free MIP signal as a reference point.

A) Cross-reactivity assays for cTnI against transferrin, BSA, NSE, glucose, AdV-Ep, and cTnI-Ep5 of each DA-nanocomposite-based electrochemical sensor. Protein images source: The Protein Data Bank (RCSB PDB) [45]. B) Epitope specificity analysis of DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor, which exhibited superior binding for Ep 4 due to its complementary cavities in the polymer matrix. C) SWV curves for cTnI protein rebinding assays in serum for the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor and D) corresponding sensor response for concentration-dependent cTnI in the range of 0.01–10 ng mL−1.
AFM roughness values obtained from 1 µm2 height images of the molecular imprinting process in air.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study integrates dopamine (DA) electropolymerization, epitope imprinting, and nanomaterials (i.e., gold nanoparticles (AuNP) and graphene quantum dots (GQD)) to develop three distinct electrochemical sensors (DA-AuNP-MIP, DA-GQD-MIP, and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP) for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI) with assessing their sensitivity, selectivity, and specificity profiles. The sensors exhibited high affinity for the target biomarker, enabling detection within a concentration range of 0.01–20 ng mL−1, with dissociation constants ranging from 2.7 to 69 pM. The successful fabrication of sensors was also confirmed through atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), fluorescence microscopy, contact angle measurements, cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The cTnI selectivity assessment of imprinted nanosensors compared to their non-imprinted counterparts at 0.01–2.5 ng mL−1 showed that the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor yielded a maximum imprinting factor of 5.9. A negligible cross-reactivity for DA-AuNP-MIP and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensors was found against glucose, adenovirus epitope, another cTnI epitope, bovine serum albumin (BSA), transferrin, and neuron-specific enolase (NSE). Epitope specificity at various concentrations (10–60 nM), concentration-dependent cTnI detection in human serum (0.01–10 ng mL−1) and sensor recovery rates (0.1–10 ng mL−1) in human serum were determined with the best-performing sensor (i.e., DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP). The sensor demonstrated very high epitope specificity in comparison to another cTnI epitope as well as excellent detection performances in human serum studies. The newly engineered epitope-mediated electrochemical sensing platform showcases considerable potential for precise and straightforward diagnosis of coronary artery diseases, aiming to materialize a cost-effective Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: When the blood supply to the coronary arteries is disrupted, it causes cell hypoxia and necrosis, leading to acute myocardial infarction (AMI) [1,2]. Biomarkers are measurable biological parameters used to assess health [1,3,4]. Cardiac troponin I (cTnI) is a circulating biomarker associated with coronary artery diseases (CAD) and is mainly used to predict cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk in secondary prevention settings [5].

The necessity for precise, specific, and sensitive detection of biomarkers to predict recurrent cardiovascular events, subsequent myocardial infarction, and cardiovascular death has spurred the development of high sensitivity-cTnI assays. The cardiac troponin complex (cTn) comprises three subdivisions - cardiac troponin C (cTnC), cardiac troponin T (cTnT), and cardiac troponin I (cTnI) - that serve as the regulatory system for the heart's contractile unit [1,3,4]. In instances of cardiomyocyte injury, cTn is released into the circulation. However, cardiac troponin I (cTnI) levels rise more rapidly in AMI, often reaching peak concentrations in circulation up to ten times higher than cTnT. Notably, cTnT tends to remain bound to tropomyosin with a higher degree of degradation, resulting in only a small proportion reaching the circulation [5].

The current accumulated evidence suggests that cTnT indicates general cardiovascular illness, affected to an even stronger degree by renal dysfunction, whereas cTnI is an independent, strong predictor of CAD [5], and more sensitive to myocardial ischemia [6]. It has also been reported that a combination testing of cTnT and cTnI might be an option to improve risk prediction in stable CAD populations but will inevitably increase the complexity of result interpretation and is, for this reason, not recommended [6]. However, the cutoff concentrations signaling a potential myocardial infarction for cTnI is 0.03–0.04 ng mL−1 [4,7,8]. Hence, the current study explores the development of a highly sensitive cTnI assay for predicting CAD risk.

Despite the availability of troponin-sensing devices in the market, they tend to be expensive. Furthermore, the sensitivity of point-of-care testing (POCT) capabilities is generally lower compared to laboratory assays. Consequently, the objective of developing ultrasensitive, highly selective, cost-effective POCT-based devices with rapid detection and user-friendly operation has not yet been accomplished [4,8,9]. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), serving as plastic antibodies, exhibit notable affinity, specificity, and selectivity for target molecules. MIPs outperform conventional affinity materials in cost-effectiveness and ease of preparation. They are typically synthesized by the polymerization of specific monomers around a target template, followed by template removal (TR) to generate binding cavities that mirror the template's size, shape, and chemical groups [9,10]. MIPs are recognized for their stability under harsh environments, broad application range, and compatibility with developing portable sensors [9,11–15].

The initial report on the electrochemical oxidation of DA to form a polydopamine (PDA) film for sensing applications was presented by Liu et al., who demonstrated its use in the imprinting of nicotine, a small molecule [12]. Building upon this, efforts were made to extend this approach to large biomolecules, including bacteria [16,17] and proteins [11,13,18,19]. However, imprinting large biomolecules faces challenges owing to several reasons: (i) the difficulties in removing templates due to their large size, (ii) the dynamic nature of biomolecules hindering polymerization, (iii) their complexity, (iv) their susceptibility to degradation in various solvents, among several others [9,14,20]. Partial or epitope imprinting presents a robust alternative approach to whole molecule imprinting for capturing large compounds like proteins. To tackle the aforementioned problems, an effective strategy is utilizing a partial imprinting approach using peptide templates through the formation of self-assembled monolayers (SAM) on surfaces before initiating the molecular imprinting process [9,21,22].

cTnI, comprised of 206 amino acids, is susceptible to proteolytic degradation. Nevertheless, Katrukha et al. identified the central regime of cTnI, spanning amino acid residues (AAR) 30–110, as the most stable segment [23]. This segment is protected against proteolytic degradation due to TnC binding [24]. Notably, there are three isoforms of TnI: cardio-specific cTnI and two skTnI variants expressed in skeletal muscle. These three proteins are homologous, and the sequence identity between cTnI and skTnI ranges from 46 % to 52 % [24]. Thus, a very short fragment of cTnI is unique, presenting a challenge in developing antibodies highly specific to cTnI without cross-reactivity, a necessity to prevent false positives. Currently, most of the high-sensitivity cTnI detection assays are developed against epitopes from the central regime of cTnI, mainly within three amino acid regions: 23–43, 41–56, and 83–93 [24]. However, highly sensitive commercial antibody assays require one or more capture-detection pairs to achieve specific binding. The combination of pairs also changes with the choice of detection platform, such as ELISA or lateral flow assay, introducing variability and making the process laborious and costly.

Here, we develop MIPs as a stand-alone receptor highly specific to cTnI binding. To circumvent the challenges associated with cross-reactivity and whole molecule imprinting, an epitope of cTnI: AAR 40–49 (referred as Ep4) has been chosen as the template in the imprinting process [21,25,26]. The reduced template size enables easier removal, reduced complexity, and enhanced binding affinity and selectivity, along with notable advantages in terms of synthesis cost [25]. Additionally, the peptide AAR 82–91 (referred as Ep5) - a commonly chosen and competing peptide from the central regime for cTnI, used in commercial assays - has been chosen as a cross-reactant to assess the target epitope specificity of the developed molecular imprints.

The use of epitope imprinting with DA as a functional monomer has been explored for cTnT [27], resulting in satisfactory sensitivity; however, the detected levels exceeded those typically found in human blood. Similarly, the epitope approach was tested for imprinting cTnI using norepinephrine, a neurotransmitter structurally analogous to DA [28]. Nonetheless, the self-polymerization of the monomer was time-consuming, and the sensor sensitivity needed to be augmented through enzyme amplification. The integration of smart nanomaterials into MIPs, coupled with electrochemistry, has garnered considerable attention in the sensing field, driven by its enhanced sensitivity properties. Zuo et al. pioneered the development of an electrochemical sensor based on MIPs to monitor cTnI in human serum. This label-free biosensor enabled the detection of cTnI within a limited range of 0.05–5 nM and had a detection time exceeding 15 minutes [29]. Mansuriya and Altintas investigated the enhancement of sensor performance, specifically the sensitivity towards cTnI, using multiple smart nanomaterials [30]. They developed an enzymeless electrochemical immunosensor that leveraged the combined features of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [31,32] and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [10] for cTnI detection. The achieved detection ranges in buffer and human serum were 1–1000 and 10–1000 pg mL−1, with calculated limits of detection (LODs) of 0.1 and 0.5 pg mL−1, respectively. The detection times ranged from 10.5 to 13 minutes [30]. Nevertheless, it is important to note that the implemented sensor necessitates the use of disposable electrodes and antibody receptors, which incur significantly higher costs compared to MIP-based sensors.

To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first investigation where partial imprinting, facile PDA electrodeposition, and the properties of smart nanomaterials were integrated to create highly efficient MIP sensors for diagnosing CAD. Three distinct electrochemically formed sensors based on DA were fabricated, each decorated with different nanomaterials (i.e., GQD, AuNP, or a combination of both), as shown in Fig. 1. Comprehensive investigations were carried out to assess the sensitivity, selectivity, and specificity profiles of each sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
ce:section.ce:para: All reagents and chemicals used in this study, along with the cleaning protocols for gold wires and gold-coated silicon wafers, are comprehensively described in the Supporting Information. Detailed synthesis procedures of peptides (Table S1, Fig. S1) and AuNPs, the parameters involved in electrochemical measurements (Table S2), and related equipment are also provided in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of smart nanomaterials embedded MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical measurements and characterization were conducted using a PalmSens4 compact electrochemical interface equipped with a three-electrode configuration. The setup consisted of an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a platinum-coiled counter electrode, and a working gold electrode. These electrodes were assembled in a static 2 mL glass cell (Fig. S2). The electrochemical interface was controlled using PSTrace5.8 software (PalmSens, Houten, Netherlands).

The bare surface served as the reference point for each surface modification step. Thus, prior to the template pre-adsorption, cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) measurements as well as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed in the presence of a redox marker (10 mM ferrocyanide in 0.1 M KCl). The template used in this study was a cysteine-modified epitope of cTnI (Cys-Ep4, AAR 40–49: H-CISASRKLQLK-OH). A final concentration of 25 µM Cys-Ep4 solution (100 µL) was mixed with PBS-TCEP buffer (1900 µL). The gold wire was incubated in the prepared template solution for 15 min, facilitating the formation of a self-assembled monolayer of the template. After the incubation period, the surface was rinsed with distilled water to remove any loosely bound peptides, and the resulting surface was characterized using voltammetric techniques.

After the formation of the template monolayer, a 10 M DA solution was prepared by vigorously mixing 2.37 mg of DA hydrochloride with 1.25 mL of PBS buffer. This DA solution was further diluted into the corresponding polymerization mixtures. Three types of sensors were fabricated, each incorporating PDA and different smart nanomaterials: DA-GQD-MIP, DA-AuNP-MIP, and DA-GQDs-AuNP-MIP. For each sensor, a specific polymerization solution was prepared fresh before each experiment. The resulting polymers contained 5 mM DA, 50 ppm GQD and/or 3.5 × 1013 NPs mL–1 AuNPs (20 × diluted), respectively. It is important to note that all these solutions should be stored in the freezer if they have not been used within 30–45 min of preparation.

The electrodeposition process was conducted using optimized CV polymerization conditions, which involved 15 cycles, a voltage window of +0.5 to −0.5 V, and a scan rate of 0.02 V s−1 (Table S2). The best electrochemical results were obtained using a 5 mM DA concentration in combination with either a 20-fold diluted solution of AuNP, a GQD concentration of 50 ppm, or a combination of both nanomaterials. The electropolymerization of DA in the presence of oxygen was evidenced by the change in color from colorless to salmon-brown, further confirmed through electrochemical measurements. The anodic desorption of the cysteine-modified template was accomplished using multistep amperometry (MA) with a 1.2 V anodic potential applied for 2 cycles. The effectiveness of each anodic potential cycle application was investigated using CV and SWV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM analysis of smart nanomaterials embedded MIPs and corresponding control polymers
ce:section.ce:para: Gold-coated silicon wafers served as substrates for atomic force microscopy (AFM) characterization. The surfaces of interest, including the bare surface, SAM formation, and templated-MIPs with DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD, as well as template-free MIPs and non-imprinted polymers (NIPs), were thoroughly examined. AFM measurements encompassed 2-D height imaging, 3-D surface topography, phase imaging, root mean square (RMS) roughness analysis, and cross-section profiling. These measurements were performed using an AFM NanoWizard II (JPK Instruments AG., Berlin, Germany) equipped with a TAP300 GD-G probe from Budget Sensors (Innovative Solutions Bulgaria Ltd., Bulgaria), operating in intermittent contact mode at room temperature. The used cantilevers had a resonance frequency in the range of 300 ± 100 kHz. Scanning was conducted at a line rate of 0.2–0.5 Hz, and 1 μm2 scanning areas were analyzed. The acquired images were processed using JPKSPM Data Processing software (version 6.1.131).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM analysis of templated -MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the imprinted and non-imprinted surfaces was studied using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). The measurements were carried out with the Gemini SEM Ultra55 Plus model from Zeiss, Germany. The MIP and NIP surfaces of DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD were prepared as per optimized parameters. The samples underwent gold sputtering (Vac Coat Desk Sputter and carbon Coater DSCR; I = 10 mA, time = 30 seconds) in an argon plasma medium to make them conducting before analysis. The SEM micrographs were acquired with an acceleration voltage of 10 KV, at magnifications of 10,000X and 100,000X, using Secondary Electron (SE2) and In-Lens detectors to reveal the structural characteristics supporting sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle measurement and fluorescence microscopy imaging of nanomaterial embedded MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The water contact angle was measured at the stage-by-stage preparation of MIPs and NIPs for DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD at multiple phases of their preparation in static mode. For each sample, contact angle was measured using an OCA 50 Micro goniometer (DataPhysics Instruments GmbH, Germany) equipped with an electronic picoliter dosing system and camera (2048 × 1088-pixel resolution). The measurement was done by dropping ∼5 µL Milli-Q water onto the sample film. Subsequently, the acquired data were analyzed with the software SCA 20 provided by DataPhysics. Fluorescence microscopic image analysis was performed on the MIP and NIP samples using Keyence BZ-X800 instrument with 20x objectives lenses (Keyence GmbH, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection assays for peptide and cTnI protein
ce:section.ce:para: Peptide recognition experiments were conducted in PBS buffer (pH 7.4), using a concentration range from 0.01 to 5.0 µM. The sensors were incubated in a solution containing the unmodified epitope (Ep4, AAR 40–49: H-ISASRKLQLK-OH), followed by SWV measurements. The detection of cTnI in PBS (pH 7.4) was also evaluated, spanning a concentration range of 0.01–20 ng mL−1. Optimum incubation times were 45, 30, and 15 min for the DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD sensors, respectively. The relative signal suppression was calculated by comparing the response of TR to the reference point for each sensor. Overall concentration-dependent analyses were carried out and demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, cross-reactivity and epitope specificity studies
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the three sensors was investigated by comparing each MIP to its corresponding NIP. The fabrication process for the NIPs followed the same steps as the MIPs, except for excluding the SAM formation step. To evaluate the specificity of the DA-based sensors, various cross-reactants such as neuron-specific enolase (NSE), bovine serum albumin (BSA), transferrin, an adenovirus epitope (AdV-Ep; AAR: AKLTLVLTKCGSQILATVSVLA), another cTnI epitope (Ep5, AAR 82–91: LELAGLGFAE), and D(+) glucose were tested against the target protein (cTnI). All analytes were prepared at a fixed concentration of 60 ng mL−1 in PBS solution and incubated with individual MIP sensors at room temperature. Following the incubation period, SWV measurements were performed. The relative signal suppression was calculated by comparing the response of TR to the reference point, and all results were compared [22]. To examine the epitope-specific binding, a rebinding assay was performed with the unmodified epitope (Ep4) and another cTnI epitope (Ep5). The detection range for both epitopes was selected as 10–60 nM in PBS (pH 7.4), with incubation at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of cTnI in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Recovery studies were conducted in human serum (50 % diluted with PBS) to assess the applicability of the DA-based sensors in real samples and their detection capacity. The individual MIP sensors were initially incubated with serum alone, and their responses were measured by SWV to obtain a reference point. Following this, the MIP sensors were incubated in serum with cTnI concentration of 0.05 ng mL−1, and subsequently, three different concentrations of cTnI (0.05, 0.95, and 9.95 ng mL−1) were added to the sensor cell. The total contained concentration, including the added cTnI, yielded concentrations of 0.1, 1.0, and 10.0 ng mL−1, and the relative signal for each concentration was obtained similarly. Following recovery analysis, a concentration-dependent cTnI detection assay in human serum (50 % diluted with PBS) was carried out. For this, a notably low cTnI concentration range of 0.01–10 ng mL−1 was chosen to evaluate the sensor performance in real samples using SWV measurements. All serum experiments were conducted while respecting the protein incubation times corresponding to each sensor type.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Synthesis of smart nanomaterials embedded MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization studies for three different MIP nanocomposites involved the investigation of various parameters, including (i) template concentration, (ii) incubation time for template adsorption on the gold surface, (iii) TR conditions, and (iv) polymerization technique. Based on the results obtained from these preliminary studies, the appropriate polymerization method was determined, and additional parameters were adjusted, such as monomer concentration, polymerization cycles, applied voltage range, and scan rate. Furthermore, the inclusion of AuNPs and GQDs at different concentrations was examined to identify the optimal content of nanocomposites that would enhance the sensing performance.

To facilitate the formation of uniform binding sites and minimize non-specific interactions, the peptide template was pre-adsorbed onto the gold surface utilizing the strong affinity between thiol groups present in cysteine residues and gold [9,22,25]. The cysteine modification of the epitope allowed its immobilization through the creation of a SAM [26]. The template concentration is a critical parameter in achieving a highly sensitive MIP, as it directly influences the number of binding sites within the final imprinted matrix [18]. In this study, two template concentrations (25 µM and 50 µM) were evaluated at various incubation times. Results showed that a concentration of 25 µM with an incubation time of 15 min yielded the optimal combination, resulting in a relative suppression range of 85–95 % as determined by SWV.

Electropolymerization provides a targeted and direct deposition of DA onto the electrode surface. Unlike the self-random dispersion in the surrounding medium, PDA readily absorbs onto the electrode surface when an appropriate potential is applied [22]. Two techniques commonly employed for DA electrodeposition are MA and CV [33]. Achieving an optimal film thickness is crucial for effective TR and subsequent target binding in these techniques. Films that are too thin interact with only a limited portion of the template, resulting in inadequate target recognition and potential detachment during subsequent steps. Conversely, excessively thick layers inadvertently trap the template, leading to an incomplete TR [22,34,35]. The results showed that a DA concentration of 5 mM and 15 CV cycles yielded the most favorable outcome. A voltage window of +0.5 to −0.5 V was chosen based on previous reports proving successful PDA film formation [12,36,37]. These studies also highlighted the influence of scan rate on DA polymerization [18,38]. Slower scan rates are believed to enhance the formation of a more robust film by allowing the cyclization reaction kinetics to proceed effectively [38]. Consequently, a scan rate of 0.02 V s−1 was deemed more suitable than 0.05 V s−1. In addition, pH variations can alter the binding affinity between the template and MIPs [14,33]. Hence, a constant pH of 7.4 was maintained throughout all the performed assays.

In this study, the TR from the MIPs was accomplished by applying an anodic potential using the MA method. During this process, the thiol groups attached to the gold surface were oxidized, leading to the release of the cysteine-modified epitopes [26]. Subsequent washings were carried out to ensure the elimination of any loosely bound residues on the MIP surface [9,22]. The chosen potential for TR was 1.2 V. On average, two cycles were necessary, as determined based on the fabricated sensors, to establish a suitable voltage window for subsequent rebinding assays ( Fig. 2).

In the field of medical diagnosis, nanomaterial-based MIP sensors have emerged as a superior alternative to conventional sensors due to their enhanced sensitivity, rapid response, and improved accuracy. This study focused on investigating different compositions of smart nanomaterials. The incorporation of DA polymerization with 20 times diluted AuNPs, 50 ppm GQDs, and the combination of both yielded the best results. The maximum conductivity observed through CV and SWV characterization was found in the bare surfaces (Fig. 2). The presence of a SAM on the surface acted as an insulating layer, leading to a decrease in the current signal. Subsequent polymerization resulted in significant suppression of the sensor signal. These findings are consistent with the reduced electron transfer between the redox marker and the gold surface. Besides, in all voltammograms (Fig. 2), the signals corresponding to the template-free MIP showed notable increments compared to their corresponding templated MIP polymers. In addition to voltammetric techniques, EIS was performed to characterize the sensor fabrication steps (Fig. S3). The EIS data of all three sensors was analyzed by plotting Nyquist plots, and fitted to a simplified Randles circuit, which contained a charge transfer resistance (Rct), a constant phase element (CPE), and a solution resistance (Rs) [39]. After DA electropolymerization, the Rct values increased significantly compared to those observed on the template-adsorbed electrode surface. This substantial increase in Rct suggests that the MIP film was successfully formed on the electrode. Subsequently, after the template removal (TR) steps, the Rct values decreased distinctly, indicating a significant increase in the current through the WE [40].

Voltammograms obtained during DA electropolymerization (Fig. 2, represented by the lowermost line in all graphs) exhibit a distinct decrease in current and reveal several redox peaks. An oxidation peak is observed around +0.3 V, accompanied by two reduction peaks around +0.1 V and −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Li et al. proposed an ECECEE reaction mechanism to explain the process of DA electropolymerization. According to this mechanism, DA undergoes oxidation to form dopaminequenone (DAQ), which corresponds to the peak observed at +0.3 V. The reduction of DAQ may be responsible for the peak at +0.1 V, and its intermolecular cyclization results in the formation of leucodopaminechrome (LDAC). The oxidation peak observed around −0.2 or −0.3 V is likely associated with the formation of dopaminechrome (DAC), while its reduction leads to a peak near −0.3 V. DAC undergoes isomerization to form 5,6-dihydroxylindole, which is in equilibrium with 5,6-indolequinone. The final product is a poly(5,6-indolequinone) film [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Surface characterization plays a crucial role in the development of electrochemical MIP sensors. In addition to CV and SWV methods, imaging techniques are employed to obtain topological information about the different stages of sensor fabrication, which deliver information about the electrochemical behavior of the films. Tapping mode-AFM (TM-AFM) was utilized to analyze surface topographies, RMS roughness, 3D height images, cross-sectional profiles, and phase images at a 1 µm2 area. The surface modifications and various chemical interactions occurring at each fabrication stage are visually captured through the significant changes observed in the corresponding phase images ( Fig. 3).

Despite the comparable roughness observed in Fig. S4 for the bare surface (RMS: 0.70 ± 0.04 nm) and the SAM (0.73 ± 0.05 nm), there are substantial differences in the cross-sectional height profiles, phase degrees, and images. These distinct characteristics evidenced that a chemically unique and uniform layer of epitope molecules was successfully formed on the electrode surface before the electrodeposition of the polymerization mixture containing functional monomers and nanomaterials.

Fig. S5A illustrates the distinct stages of modification involved in the fabrication of the DA-AuNP-MIP sensor. Notably, the phase shifts demonstrate the varying morphologies of the sensor surface throughout the molecular imprinting process. The absence of template molecules results in a smoother surface, as evidenced by a 0.40 nm difference in roughness between the MIP and NIP surfaces.

The modification steps involved in the fabrication of the DA-GQD-MIP sensor are depicted in Fig. S5B, where distinct phase shifts are observed between surfaces, indicating different interactions with the AFM tip. Significant differences can be observed in the height and phase images of the template-free MIP (TF-MIP in figure) and NIP surfaces. Firstly, the TF-MIP surface exhibits a greater height range and roughness, indicating the presence of significant surface heterogeneity attributed to the formation of cavities. Secondly, the TF-MIP phase image reveals a uniform distribution of seemingly small pores, while the NIP surface displays a homogenous pattern of structures scattered on a flatter surface. Both characteristics are discernible in the height images. The templated MIP (T-MIP in figure) surface exhibits evident arrays of particles, creating a rougher topology as reflected in a higher RMS value (0.98 ± 0.06 nm) and smaller intervals in the cross-section profile. However, the T-MIP surface maintains a smaller height range than the TF-MIP surface.

The characterization steps involved in the surface modification of DA-AuNP-GQD are presented in Fig. 4. The height and phase images of the T-MIP surface appear relatively homogeneous but exhibit the highest RMS value (1.43 ± 0.05 nm), height range, and phase shift (129°) among all the sensors. Recurrent higher peaks can be observed across the cross-section profile. The substantial differences in material properties across the surface, as evidenced by the significant phase shift, may be attributed to the presence of two different nanoparticles. These nanoparticles contribute to the formation of a unique polymer matrix surrounding the template, resulting in distinct mechanical properties. In contrast, the NIP surface lacks the formation of cavities, leading to different surface characteristics. The height image of the TF-MIP surface also reveals an interesting surface morphology characterized by greater and more frequent indentations as reflected in the cross-section profile.

The RMS values reported in the literature for PDA films typically range from approximately 1.5 to 5.0 nm, with variations depending on the electrodeposition method and the number of cycles used to produce the films [33,37,41]. Notably, the current method employed in this study differs by including oxygen in the polymer mix and omitting the oxygen removal step. Zhao et al. achieved a smoother PDA film (RMS: 0.78 nm) by implementing a fast polymerization process catalyzed with CuSO4/H2O2 on Au-coated silicon wafers [42]. In this work, the RMS values of the corresponding MIP films ranged from 0.6 to 1.5 nm ( Table 1), which aligns with results obtained using electro- and self-polymerized methods. However, it is important to note that the smoothness of the depositing surface and the SAM significantly affect film homogeneity [42]. Furthermore, the roughness values of the NIP films were lower than those of the corresponding MIP films (Table 1), confirming that the absence of a template in the polymer network results in a smoother surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine polymerized MIPs and NIPs were characterized by high-resolution SEM to analyze the surface morphology and structural modifications by incorporating nanomaterials, as illustrated in Fig. 5. For DA-AuNP-GQD surfaces, a combination of In-Lens and SE detectors was used, while in the case of DA-AuNP and DA-GQD surfaces, In-Lens detector was used to obtain sharp images.

In the SEM micrograph of DA-GQD-MIP (Fig. 5A), a few isolated pyramidal microscopic structures are observed, which appeared to be localized agglomerated GQDs, distributed unequally over the dopamine film. In contrast, the dispersed nature of the AuNPs is an appealing aspect of DA-AuNP-MIP (Fig. 5B). The white flecks in the grayish background represent AuNPs. This observation aligns with the highest roughness RMS value of DA-AuNP-MIP in AFM analysis, likely attributed to the microscopic AuNP structures uniformly distributed in DA film. Fig. 5C displays a homogenous distribution of nanomaterials over the thicker, amorphous DA film for DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP.

Similarly, SEM micrographs, Fig. 5D to F, reveal morphological variations in the NIP films of dopamine. In the case of DA-GQD-NIP (Fig. 5D), a thick sheet/film without agglomerated GQDs is evident. For DA-AuNP-NIP in Fig. 5E, a thin amorphous sheet is observed with a non-homogenous distribution of AuNPs. The surface topology of DA-AuNP-GQD-NIP (Fig. 5F) significantly differs from its MIP counterpart. It displayed an amorphous sheet with non-uniform microcrystals of nanomaterials, suggesting the significance of cysteine-modified epitopes. The inhomogeneous distribution of nanomaterials in NIPs may be attributed to the absence of Au-S linkages, which are present in MIP films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle measurement and fluorescence microscopic imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Surface hydrophilicity was assessed for each functionalized surface using a contact angle meter. Fig. 6(A-H) illustrate the goniometer pictures captured directly by dropping water onto the film surfaces. Fig. 6I shows the contact angles obtained from the goniometer pictures. As anticipated, the bare surface, exhibited the highest hydrophobicity, while SAM demonstrated the highest hydrophilicity due to the presence of cysteine-modified epitope of cTnI on its surface. When comparing MIPs and NIPs of DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD, it is noteworthy that NIPs displayed greater hydrophobicity than MIPs. The trend is the same in DA-AuNP, DA-GQD, and DA-AuNP-GQD surfaces.

Fluorescence microscopy provides a non-invasive high-resolution imaging of nanoscale materials in a sample with fluorescence properties. In Fig. S6 A, C and E, we observed that DA-AuNP-MIP, DA-GQD-MIP and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP containing cysteine modified epitope of cTnI, respectively, exhibited significantly higher fluorescence compared to their corresponding NIPs. The presence of cTnI peptide appears to be the key factor contributing to the strong agglomeration observed with GQDs and AuNPs during electropolymerization of dopamine. The particular network structure of MIP films, the quantum confinement of nanoparticles and the recombination of fluorescence might have collectively contributed to the high agglomerate-induced fluorescence. In the case of NIP films (Fig. S6 B, D, and F), the images show negligible fluorescence, which infers the significance of template molecules in the imprinting process. As the template epitope (Cys-Ep4) is absent in NIPs, the nanomaterials couldn’t establish a concrete network with DA polymeric film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of cTnI peptide and protein
ce:section.ce:para: The three DA-based imprinted nanosensors were subjected to target epitope and protein detection assays to evaluate their efficacy and binding properties. The affinity of the sensors was assessed by determining their dissociation constants (Kd), which were calculated by fitting the binding isotherms with specific adsorption models (i.e., Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir-Freundlich (LF) isotherms) proposed by Umpleby et al. [43]. Furthermore, the sensitivity of each sensor was determined by establishing the lowest detectable concentration or limit of detection (LOD).

A wide range of epitope concentrations (0.01–5.0 µM) was tested with each DA-based sensor to examine their binding behavior ( Fig. 7A). All sensors successfully detected Ep4 at the lowest concentration (LOD = 0.01 µM) and showed the best fit to the LF isotherm (Tables S4 and S5). The binding behavior of the sensors revealed a combination of heterogeneous and homogeneous binding sites within the polymer cavities. The analysis also indicated that the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor had the dissociation constant, suggesting it was more prone to releasing the captured epitope compared to the DA-AuNP-MIP sensor (Fig. 7B). However, the differences in dissociation constants were within the same order of magnitude (Table S5).

Distinct binding characteristics appear to arise from the different nanocomposite polymer matrixes and the time required for each sensor to detect the protein also varies across the sensing platforms ( Fig. 8). For the DA-AuNP sensor, an optimal binding time of 45 min was determined, as it exhibited the highest level of subsequent signal suppression when increasing the concentration of cTnI (Fig. 8, pink scale). In contrast, the DA-GQD sensor required a 30-min incubation with the protein prior to electrochemical sensing, yielding the most consistent increase in signal suppression with each varying concentration (Fig. 8, orange stripes). Lastly, the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor achieved protein detection with the shortest incubation time of 15 min, and the binding profile did not show any improvement with an extended incubation period (Fig. 8, blue dots).

Protein binding behavior was further investigated in the concentration range of 0.01–20 ng mL−1 using all three DA-based sensors ( Fig. 9A and S7). The DA-AuNP-MIP sensor demonstrated the ability to detect troponin at a concentration of 0.05 ng mL−1. In contrast, both GQD-containing sensors could detect the protein at a lower concentration of 0.01 ng mL−1. Regarding the binding response, the Freundlich model exhibited a higher R2 value in all cases, indicating a pronounced heterogeneous behavior of the recognition layer when interacting with the entire protein (Fig. 9B). Moreover, the Freundlich model provided a better fit for the binding curve of the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor. It is worth noting that the DA-AuNP-MIP sensor exhibited the highest dissociation constant, while the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor displayed lower susceptibility to release the target protein once it was captured (Fig. 9C). The difference in Kd values between AuNP-MIP and GQD-MIP can be attributed to the intrinsic properties and specific interactions of AuNPs and GQDs with the sensing layer. GQDs appear to possess multiple reactive sites, facilitating stable functionalization and the formation of complex structures. This might have resulted in an MIP layer with a greater and more uniform distribution of cavities, enhancing target binding and thus leading to lower Kd values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, cross-reactivity and epitope specificity studies
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the imprinted nanosensors was investigated by comparing them with their non-imprinted counterparts. The cTnI selectivity of the DA-GQD-MIP and DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensors was studied in the concentration range of 0.01–2.5 ng mL−1, resulting in imprinting factors (IFs) of 2.0 and 5.9, respectively (Fig. S8). Despite demonstrating promising sensitivity and selectivity profiles, the DA-GQD-MIP sensor exhibited significantly lower IF, which could be attributed to the non-selective interaction of GQDs with the protein. This emphasizes the importance of achieving an ideal nanocomposite formation with desirable sensing properties. To further investigate the selectivity of the DA-AuNP-MIP sensor, various non-specific molecules, and the target biomarker were tested on both MIP and NIP surfaces (Fig. S9). The binding behavior of non-specific compounds showed negligible signal differences between the imprinted and non-imprinted layers, whereas cTnI binding was four-fold higher on the MIP surface compared to the NIP surface. The chemical and physical properties of PDA, including adsorption properties, are expected to depend on the redox state of its functional groups [33]. Moreover, the as-synthesized AuNPs used in the sensor were stabilized by citrate molecules, resulting in a negatively charged outer shell dominated by carboxylic groups [44]. This could explain the enhanced selectivity observed in the DA-AuNP-MIP sensor. GQDs, being zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a structure analogous to graphene, tend to form layered structures [30,32]. While this favors film formation and results in a more robust polymer film, the inclusion of GQDs may also reduce electrostatic repulsion and enable strong π-π interactions with non-specific reactants, leading to a less selective response in the DA-GQD-MIP sensor.

The specificity of a sensor towards the target biomarker is crucial, considering the presence of numerous interfering molecules in human blood that can potentially lead to false positives due to undesired interactions with the sensing system. To investigate the specificity of the three distinct nanosensors, glucose (a small molecule), adenovirus, and other epitopes (AdV-Ep and cTnI-Ep5), and three different proteins (BSA, transferrin, and NSE) were tested at a fixed concentration of 60 ng mL−1. SWV measurements were recorded for each nanosensor, and the relative signal of the redox marker for each sample was calculated for each sample ( Fig. 10A). Minimal binding was observed for the epitopes in all the sensors, indicating the epitope-specificity of the imprinted cavities. BSA, transferrin, and NSE showed slightly higher affinity towards the nanosensors due to their similarities in nature with the cTnI protein. Overall, the lowest cross-reactivity was observed with DA-AuNP-MIP, followed by DA-GQD-AuNP-MIP and DA-GQD-MIP, respectively. It is worth noting that the contribution of GQD to the robustness of the polymer layer is also reflected in the dissociation constants obtained in the rebinding assays. The DA-AuNP-GQD sensor exhibited a greater affinity for cTnI, benefiting from both the selectivity of AuNPs and the enhanced stability provided by GQDs in the polymer matrix.

Further, epitope specificity was re-examined for the best MIP sensor, DA-AuNP-GQD, that exhibited greater cTnI affinity. For this, a rebinding assay with unmodified Ep4 (AAR 40–49) and Ep5 (another cTnI epitope, AAR 82–91) was studied using SWV. Experiments were performed by incubating the sensor with each concentration of epitopes from 10 nM to 60 nM in PBS for 15 minutes. For each sample, the relative signal of the redox marker was determined from SWV measurements (Fig. 10B). The data demonstrated a minimal binding for Ep5 as the signals at high concentrations showed an overall reduction in suppression. At the same time, the rebinding assay with targeted template Ep 4 demonstrated a substantial rise in relative signal suppression, confirming that the sensor's cavities are exclusively complementary to the template used for MIP synthesis. Hence, the imprinted cavities in DA-AuNP-GQD MIP sensor are highly cTnI epitope specific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of cTnI in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the accuracy and applicability of the developed sensors, recovery assays were conducted in human serum, which was also employed as the blank base measurement. Initially, a known concentration of cTnI was detected, followed by the detection of another solution to achieve a final concentration (Ct) of 0.1, 1.0, and 10 ng mL−1 ( Table 2). The recovery percentages for the sensors were determined to be 138, 187, and 131 % for DA-AuNP-MIP; 102, 122, and 106 % for DA-GQD-MIP; and 102, 103, and 99 % for the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor. Notably, the recovery rates obtained with the latter sensor fell within the recommended range of 80–110 % suggested by the Association of Analytical Communities-AOAC [46]. This indicates that the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor holds promising potential for practical applications in human sample analysis.

Additionally, the performance of the DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor in human serum was evaluated with very low cTnI concentrations to assess its efficacy in clinical scenarios. To accomplish this, the sensor was immersed in a solution containing different concentrations of cTnI protein from 0.01–10 ng mL−1, dissolved in a mixture of PBS (pH: 7.4) and human serum in a 50:50 ratio for a period of 15 minutes. An increase in the protein concentration resulted in a corresponding reduction in the SWV peak signal, as shown in Fig. 10C. The sensor exhibited remarkable capability in detecting the cTnI protein in complex media, achieving an LOD of 0.01 ng mL−1 with a linear response for cTnI concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 2.5 ng mL−1, as depicted in Fig. 10D. Serum sample examinations underscored the superior cTnI detection performance of the developed DA-AuNP-GQD-MIP sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we developed novel epitope-mediated electrochemical sensors using cTnI-responsive nanocomposite materials. The sensors were composed of PDA-based MIPs and nanomaterials (GQDs and AuNPs), specifically targeting a cTnI-derived peptide epitope as well as the whole biomarker. Surface imprinting was employed to synthesize the nanocomposite films in the presence of cysteine-modified epitope templates. The resulting imprinted cavities on the nanocomposite film layer exhibited the ability to detect the peptide in a concentration range of 0.01–5 µM using three distinct nanosensors (i.e., DA-GQD-MIP, DA-AuNP-MIP, and DA-GQD-AuNP-MIP sensors). Furthermore, these cavities allowed for the recognition of cTnI protein in the range of 0.01–20 ng mL−1 in both PBS and serum samples, demonstrating high affinities in the pM range. Importantly, the binding of non-specific polymers to the target molecules was very weak for DA-AuNP-MIP and DA-GQD-AuNP-MIP sensors, indicating an increased potential for POCT. In terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and specificity, the DA-GQD-AuNP-MIP sensor exhibited superior performance compared to the other two sensing platforms. The fabricated sensor, utilizing the combined contributions of MIPs, GQDs, and AuNPs, demonstrates great potential for protein recognition and detection. The superiority of this nanocomposite sensor was also confirmed with recovery and concentration-dependent assays in serum samples, indicating its applicability not only for cTnI detection for coronary artery disease prediction but also for a wide range of protein biomarkers.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Roderich Süssmuth is a full professor and the Chair of Biological Chemistry at the Technical University of Berlin. He studied chemistry and biochemistry at the Eberhard Karls University in Tübingen and obtained his Ph.D. in 1999. During a postdoctoral stay at the Scripps Research Institute in La Jolla, United States, from 2000 to 2001, he worked with Richard A. Lerner and Carlos F. Barbas on catalytically acting antibodies and organocatalysis. As an Emmy Noether Research Fellow (DFG) from 2001 to 2004, he habilitated in chemistry and biochemistry at the University of Tübingen. In 2004, he was appointed Associate Professor of Biological Chemistry at the TU Berlin. In 2008, he was appointed to the Rudolf Wiechert Professorship in Biological Chemistry at the TU Berlin. Since June 2019, he has been the spokesperson for the Research Training Group "Bioactive Peptides - Innovative Aspects of Synthesis and Biosynthesis”. Since 2016, he has been on the scientific advisory board of Pentracor GmbH (Hennigsdorf), which is developing new forms of therapy for heart attack patients and for the treatment of autoimmune diseases. In 2020, he became editor of the journal RSC Chemical Biology. He is a member of the Society of German Chemists, American Chemical Society, European Peptide Society, Max Bergmann Society, German Society for Mass Spectrometry, and the German Society for Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, as well as the Technion Society. In 2020, he became a Fellow of the Royal Society of Chemistry (FRSC).

Zeynep Altintas is a full professor and the Chair of Bioinspired Materials and Biosensor Technologies at the Kiel University, Germany. She has been the Head of Biosensors and Receptor Development Group at the Technical University of Berlin since 2016. She completed her Ph.D. on biomedical sensors at the age of 25 with the outstanding Ph.D. student award. She has authored >170 publications and supervised >35 Ph.D. and M.Sc. students and mentored 15 post-doctoral fellows. Her reputation is recognized by many prestigious international awards and grants for her research. Among others are the Life Science Bridge Award, the Life Outstanding Investigator Award for Women, the Royal Society of Chemistry Research Award, the Marie Curie Individual Fellowship for Experienced Researchers, the TUBITAK Fellowship for Internationally Recognized Scientists, the Travel Grants from the British Council (2014–2016), and several best poster, oral presentation and paper awards. She serves as a referee for several funding research institutions including the European Union (EU), the German Research Foundation (DFG), the Dutch Research Council (NWO), the Israel Science Foundation (ISF), the French National Research Agency (ANR), the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) & EU Cofund Projects, and the Wisconsin Groundwater Coordinating Council (USA). She is the associate editor of Sensing and Bio-Sensing Research, and an editorial board member of Biosensors and Bioelectronics, Scientific Reports, Micromachines, and Materials. She takes parts in the organization and scientific committees of several international conferences. She is also a member of Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) since 2012 and holds visiting professorships in various EU countries.
